attributes.stem:
A 14-month-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent with a one-day history of a new rash. The child had a high fever of 39.5°C for three days with irritability and rhinorrhoea, which resolved yesterday. There was no change in bowel habits, cough or vomiting. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. His immunisations are up to date.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a blanching, rose-pink maculopapular rash on his trunk and neck that does not appear to be itchy. The rash spares his face. There are no vesicles or petechiae.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of roseola infantum (exanthem subitum), caused by roseolovirus human herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) and less frequently HHV-7. The characteristic presentation includes high fever, fatigue and rhinorrhoea for three to five days followed by rapid defervescence and appearance of small 2-5 mm non-pruritic rose-pink maculopapular rash for one to two days. It is a common viral infection worldwide with up to 100% being seropositive by age three years.

Supportive care only is the most appropriate management as roseola is a self-limiting condition. The rash typically resolves within one to two days without any specific treatment. Parents should be reassured about the benign nature of the condition.

Oral amoxicillin is not indicated as this is a viral illness. The characteristic presentation and absence of systemic symptoms after fever resolution make a bacterial infection unlikely.

Oral antihistamines would not be beneficial as the rash is not histamine-mediated or pruritic. These medications would not affect the duration or appearance of the rash.

Topical hydrocortisone is not necessary as the rash is not inflammatory or pruritic. Corticosteroids would not alter the course of the illness and could potentially mask other developing conditions.

Oral aciclovir would not be indicated as antiviral therapy has no role in the management of roseola. While HHV-6 is a herpesvirus, aciclovir is not effective against this particular virus and the condition is self-limiting.

The key to management is recognising the classic biphasic pattern of roseola (high fever followed by rash after fever resolution) to provide appropriate reassurance and avoid unnecessary treatments. Febrile seizures may occur in 10-15% of cases. During the febrile phase, antipyretics may be used for comfort, but they do not alter the course of the illness.
learningPoints: Roseola infantum typically presents with high fever for three to five days followed by a characteristic rose-pink maculopapular rash after fever resolution.
The rash of roseola is self-limiting and requires no specific treatment, resolving within one to two days.
Febrile seizures occur in 10-15% of roseola cases during the febrile phase.
references: Pippin M, Laws G. A Classic Presentation of Roseola Infantum. Cureus. 2024 Jan 18;16(1). Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10874238/
Ngan V, Oakley A.  Roseola.  DermNet NZ.  2015. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/roseola

Ting S, Nixon R. Clinical features of viral exanthems. Aust J Gen Pract. 2021;50(4):231-236. doi:10.31128/AJGP-02-20-5246. Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2021/april/clinical-features-of-viral-exanthems
